content.text: A bridge call and war room are the nerve centre of any SEV1 or SEV2 incident. The first ten minutes determine whether your team spends the next hour moving in a coordinated direction or circling in confusion. This topic defines exactly how to stand up both artefacts — the Slack incident channel, the video bridge, and the status document — and keep them functional throughout the incident lifecycle.

attrs.alt: Bridge call and war room setup timeline showing T+0 detection through T+15 ongoing cadence, plus role assignments and channel hygiene rules

content.text: is not a physical place — it is a temporary coordination space assembled at incident declaration. It has three components:
— the persistent, searchable, asynchronous record of everything that happened
— the synchronous audio or video session where decisions are made in real-time
— a live shared doc (Notion, Confluence, or Google Doc) that the scribe keeps updated with the current hypothesis, actions, and resolution criteria
a place for diagnosis — responders do that in their own consoles and terminals. The war room is where findings are reported, decisions are made, and communication happens.
Immediately on declaring an incident, create a Slack channel following the naming convention:

#inc-YYYY-MM-DD-NNN   (e.g. #inc-2024-03-15-042)

Where NNN is the zero-padded daily incident sequence number.
For safety incidents, prefix: #inc-SAFETY-2024-03-15-001
Set the channel topic immediately so anyone who joins understands context at a glance:
[SEV1 ACTIVE] Inference API latency P99 > 30s — IC: @alice — Bridge: <zoom-link>
on-call responders, the IC, the scribe, and the comms liaison immediately. Do not add spectators until the incident is under control — noise on the bridge hurts coordination.
The IC opens the bridge call within five minutes of incident declaration. For SEV1 incidents, the IC must be the first to join and must not leave until handoff or resolution.
IC opening statement (read verbatim or adapt):

"This is the SEV1 bridge for incident 2024-03-15-042, inference API latency. The time is 14:23 UTC. I am Alice, IC. Please state your name and role when you join. We are investigating elevated P99 latency on the inference API. Our immediate goal is to understand scope and identify a mitigation path. Scribe: please open the status doc and timestamp this call start."
Post the bridge link immediately into the incident channel and pin it. Anyone who cannot join video should be able to follow along via the channel.
The IC assigns these roles explicitly on the bridge — do not assume someone has picked up a role:
Runs bridge, makes decisions, owns resolution
Timestamped log, status doc, action items
Status page, exec updates, customer-facing comms
For SEV3+ incidents, the scribe and comms liaison roles may be omitted. The IC takes on both responsibilities.
The scribe opens the status document from a template and shares it in the channel. The document must have these sections at minimum:
Incident ID, severity, IC name, start time
Resolution criteria (what must be true to declare resolved)
Timeline of key events (automatically built from scribe log)
Manual channel creation at 3 AM is error-prone. Automate it with a Slack bolt app:

from slack_sdk import WebClient
from datetime import date
import re

class WarRoomSetup:
    def __init__(self, token: str, zoom_api: ZoomClient):
        self.slack = WebClient(token=token)
        self.zoom = zoom_api

    def create_incident_channel(self, severity: str, title: str, ic_user_id: str) -> dict:
        today = date.today().isoformat()
        seq = self._next_sequence_number(today)
        channel_name = f"inc-{today}-{seq:03d}"
        if "safety" in severity.lower():
            channel_name = f"inc-safety-{today}-{seq:03d}"

        # Create channel
        channel = self.slack.conversations_create(name=channel_name, is_private=False)
        channel_id = channel["channel"]["id"]

        # Set topic
        safe_title = title[:60]
        self.slack.conversations_setTopic(
            channel=channel_id,
            topic=f"[{severity} ACTIVE] {safe_title} — IC: <@{ic_user_id}>"
        )

        # Create bridge meeting
        meeting = self.zoom.meetings.create(
            topic=f"[{severity}] {safe_title}",
            duration=240,
        )
        bridge_url = meeting["join_url"]

        # Post and pin bridge link
        msg = self.slack.chat_postMessage(
            channel=channel_id,
            text=f":rotating_light: *{severity} INCIDENT* — {title}\n"
                 f"Bridge: {bridge_url}\n"
                 f"IC: <@{ic_user_id}>"
        )
        self.slack.pins_add(channel=channel_id, timestamp=msg["ts"])

        # Invite on-call responders
        oncall = self._get_oncall_responders(severity)
        self.slack.conversations_invite(channel=channel_id, users=",".join(oncall))

        return {
            "channel_id": channel_id,
            "channel_name": channel_name,
            "bridge_url": bridge_url,
            "incident_id": f"INC-{today}-{seq:03d}".upper(),
        }

    def _next_sequence_number(self, today: str) -> int:
        result = self.slack.conversations_list(types="public_channel")
        pattern = re.compile(rf"^inc-{today}-(\d{{3}})$")
        numbers = [int(m.group(1)) for ch in result["channels"]
                   if (m := pattern.match(ch["name"]))]
        return max(numbers, default=0) + 1
Most bridge calls fail not because of missing information but because of missing structure. The IC must actively enforce these rules:
Every person joining the bridge call must state their name and role immediately. The scribe notes the join time. The IC should verbally acknowledge each joiner once and then move on.

IC: "Who just joined?"
Joiner: "This is Dave, ML platform lead."
IC: "Welcome Dave, we need you to look at inference pod restart counts."
The IC drives structured update cycles. For SEV1:
For SEV2, the cadence is every 15 minutes. For SEV3, the IC may run an async-only update loop via the channel.
Common bridge failure modes and how the IC handles them:
"Pause — summarise the finding, not the process."
"We address that in the post-mortem. What is the mitigation right now?"
"Dave, what is your current hypothesis? Thirty seconds."
"Please mute and follow the channel updates."
"We have two options. I'm picking option A. Execute. Review in 10 minutes."
The status document is the single source of truth for anyone not on the bridge. The scribe owns it and updates it every ten minutes, or whenever a significant finding lands.

# INC-2024-03-15-042 — Inference API Latency SEV1

**IC:** Alice | **Scribe:** Bob | **Comms:** Carol
**Declared:** 2024-03-15 14:20 UTC
**Bridge:** <zoom-link>

---

## Current Status
**Hypothesis:** Inference pods in us-east-1a crashing on OOM — model cache not respecting limits
**Confidence:** Medium (3/5 pods show identical OOM pattern)
**User Impact:** ~40% of inference requests timing out in us-east-1a

## Actions In Flight
| Who | What | ETA |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Dave | Cordon us-east-1a, redirect traffic | T+5 |
| Eve | Check memory limit config in Helm values | T+10 |

## Resolution Criteria
- [ ] P99 latency < 2s for 10 consecutive minutes
- [ ] Error rate < 0.1%
- [ ] All pods healthy

## Timeline
| Time UTC | Event |
| --- | --- |
| 14:18 | Alert: inference-latency-p99 > 5s |
| 14:20 | Alice declares SEV1, channel created |
| 14:25 | Bridge opened, Dave and Eve paged |
| 14:32 | Dave identifies OOM on us-east-1a pods |
The incident channel is a durable record that feeds the post-mortem. Noisy channels hide signal. Enforce these rules:
Decisions and rationale ("Decided to cordon us-east-1a at 14:35, reason: isolates blast radius")
Status updates from the bridge (posted by scribe every 10 min)
Bridge and doc links (pinned at T+0)
What Does Not Belong in the Channel
Long log dumps — link to the log search, not paste the raw output
Debugging commentary that doesn't conclude ("hmm let me check this...")
Blame or speculation about causes before data confirms
Management check-ins asking for status when a pinned doc already exists

# slack-bot config for /incident commands
slash_commands:
  /inc-update:
    description: Post a status update with automatic timestamp
    template: "[{timestamp}] STATUS UPDATE: {text}"
    also_updates_status_doc: true

  /inc-action:
    description: Assign an action item
    template: "[{timestamp}] ACTION: {assignee} — {task} (due {eta})"
    creates_jira_task: true

  /inc-hypothesis:
    description: Update current working hypothesis
    template: "[{timestamp}] HYPOTHESIS: {text} (confidence: {confidence})"
The scribe role is underrated and underspecified in most runbooks. A bad scribe produces an incomplete post-mortem. A good scribe makes the post-mortem near-automatic.
Open the status doc from template within T+10 and share in channel
Timestamp every significant event with UTC time and a brief description
Keep the "Current Hypothesis" section updated after every bridge cycle
Track all action items with owner and ETA — ask for clarification if an IC assignment lacks either
Note every decision and its rationale (this feeds the post-mortem "what we did and why" section
Post a status summary to the channel every 10 minutes for SEV1, every 15 for SEV2
At resolution, fill in the "Resolution" section before the channel is archived

14:20 UTC — IC Alice declares SEV1. Channel #inc-2024-03-15-042 created.
14:23 UTC — Bridge opened. Dave (ML), Eve (Infra), Carol (Comms) joined.
14:25 UTC — HYPOTHESIS: OOM on inference pods in us-east-1a.
14:30 UTC — ACTION: Dave to cordon us-east-1a by 14:40. Eve to check Helm memory limits by 14:35.
14:35 UTC — Eve confirms Helm memory limit set to 4Gi but pods allocated 8Gi. FINDING: config drift.
14:40 UTC — DECISION: Cordon us-east-1a and reroute traffic to us-west-2. Reason: isolates blast radius while fix is prepared.
14:45 UTC — Latency P99 dropping. 12s → 6s → 2.8s. Monitoring for 10 consecutive minutes below 2s.
14:55 UTC — P99 < 2s for 10 minutes. Error rate 0.04%. Resolution criteria met.
14:57 UTC — IC Alice declares SEV1 resolved. Bridge closed. Post-mortem scheduled 2024-03-16 10:00 UTC.
For a large SEV1 incident involving three or more functional teams (ML, Infra, Data, Security), a single bridge call becomes unmanageable above ~12 participants. Use a
The IC never joins the sub-bridges directly — the subcommanders report findings upward to the main bridge on schedule. This prevents the IC from getting drawn into debugging conversations.
A PagerDuty webhook can trigger war room setup automatically. This reduces the T+0 → T+5 window to under 90 seconds:
Responders already debugging in silos; duplicate work
Automate channel+bridge creation via PagerDuty webhook
IC explicitly assigns scribe within T+5 — never skip
IC gets lost in detail; top-level coordination dies
IC stays on main bridge; subcommanders report up
Stakeholders message IC directly for updates
Bot auto-creates doc from template at T+0
Responders can't start cleanup; people drop off without IC knowing
IC explicitly closes bridge and moves to #inc-* channel for follow-up
Anyone can invite people to bridge
Only IC grants bridge access for SEV1/SEV2
Create the incident Slack channel at T+0 with a naming convention and set the topic immediately
Open the bridge call at T+5; IC reads a structured opening statement and assigns roles explicitly
Assign IC, Scribe, Comms Liaison, and Responder roles on the bridge within T+10
Pin the bridge link, status doc, and runbook in the channel within T+5
The scribe maintains a timestamped log and updates the status doc every 10 minutes
IC enforces bridge discipline: one speaker, no blame, structured update cadence
For SEV1 with 3+ teams, use a commander-subcommander bridge structure
Automate war room setup via PagerDuty or alertmanager webhooks to eliminate manual T+0 friction
